card_objective: Explain that an LLM uses learned weights plus current context to score and generate next tokens without implying awareness, search, or permanent learning.
stem: When an LLM answers a prompt by generating one token at a time, what is the model doing at each step?
correct_choice_text: It scores possible next tokens from the current context, selects one, appends it, and repeats.
correct_feedback: Good distinction. The model is using fixed learned weights and temporary context to make the next-token step.
choice_1_text: It scores possible next tokens from the current context, selects one, appends it, and repeats.
choice_1_feedback: Good distinction. The model is using fixed learned weights and temporary context to make the next-token step.
choice_2_text: It reads with conscious understanding, then writes the whole answer at once.
choice_2_feedback: Not quite. That gives the model too much mind. Fluent behavior can happen without awareness.
choice_3_text: It searches a hidden database and copies the best matching passage.
choice_3_feedback: This choice reveals a common mix-up. Search can be added around a model, but a plain LLM generates from context and learned weights.
choice_4_text: It changes its weights each time the user sends a new prompt.
choice_4_feedback: That would be true only if a separate training process updated weights. Ordinary inference usually leaves weights fixed.
review_notes: Shorten the correct answer or lengthen/tighten distractors so the correct choice is not obviously more complete.

card_objective: Explain that an LLM uses learned weights plus current context to score and generate next tokens without implying awareness, search, or permanent learning.
stem: An LLM gives a fluent explanation of a poem. What should a model-literate learner conclude?
correct_choice_text: It generated response tokens from context and learned patterns; fluency does not prove awareness.
correct_feedback: Insight strengthened. This is the fluent-behavior-without-awareness boundary Prompt Life wants learners to hold.
choice_1_text: It generated response tokens from context and learned patterns; fluency does not prove awareness.
choice_1_feedback: Insight strengthened. This is the fluent-behavior-without-awareness boundary Prompt Life wants learners to hold.
choice_2_text: It must have inner awareness because the explanation sounds human.
choice_2_feedback: Not quite. That gives the model too much mind. Fluent behavior can happen without awareness.
choice_3_text: It must have found a hidden perfect source and copied the interpretation.
choice_3_feedback: Close, but this describes search or retrieval better than a plain LLM response. A fluent answer is not the same as cited evidence.
choice_4_text: The poem prompt permanently taught the base model a new skill.
choice_4_feedback: That would require training. A normal prompt can shape temporary context without durably updating weights.
review_notes: Shorten the correct answer or lengthen/tighten distractors so the correct choice is not obviously more complete.

card_objective: Use the AI family map to distinguish AI, ML, deep learning, generative AI, LLMs, diffusion models, multimodal systems, and AI products.
stem: A campus tool summarizes text with an LLM, while another creates images by denoising a noisy pattern. What does that show?
correct_choice_text: Generative AI is a family; LLMs and diffusion models are different branches.
correct_feedback: Good distinction. Generative AI is a family, and different branches can use different mechanisms.
choice_1_text: Generative AI is a family; LLMs and diffusion models are different branches.
choice_1_feedback: Good distinction. Generative AI is a family, and different branches can use different mechanisms.
choice_2_text: All generative AI tools are LLMs because ChatGPT is generative AI.
choice_2_feedback: Not quite. An LLM is one kind of generative AI, not the whole category.
choice_3_text: The image tool is not AI unless it generates language tokens.
choice_3_feedback: Close, but image models can be AI without using the LLM text-token loop.
choice_4_text: AI and machine learning are the same label, so the branches do not matter.
choice_4_feedback: This choice collapses useful categories. Machine learning is a major branch inside the broader AI field.
review_notes: Either replace future-stage jargon with plain language or add a brief explanation in the stem/feedback.

card_objective: Use the AI family map to distinguish AI, ML, deep learning, generative AI, LLMs, diffusion models, multimodal systems, and AI products.
stem: A department uses the words AI, machine learning, generative AI, and LLM as if they mean the same thing. Which correction keeps the category map clear?
correct_choice_text: AI is broad; machine learning learns from data; generative AI creates outputs; LLMs focus on language or code.
correct_feedback: Insight strengthened. The map matters because an LLM is one branch inside a wider AI landscape.
choice_1_text: AI is broad; machine learning learns from data; generative AI creates outputs; LLMs focus on language or code.
choice_1_feedback: Insight strengthened. The map matters because an LLM is one branch inside a wider AI landscape.
choice_2_text: All AI systems are LLMs if they use language.
choice_2_feedback: That collapses the broad AI family into one model type.
choice_3_text: Machine learning and AI are exact synonyms.
choice_3_feedback: That misses that machine learning is a major branch inside broader AI.
choice_4_text: Diffusion models and LLMs use the same generation loop.
choice_4_feedback: That treats different generative methods as if they shared one mechanism.
review_notes: Shorten the correct answer or lengthen/tighten distractors so the correct choice is not obviously more complete.

card_objective: Use the AI family map to distinguish AI, ML, deep learning, generative AI, LLMs, diffusion models, multimodal systems, and AI products.
stem: A chatbot product includes an LLM, a search tool, safety filters, and a user interface. What is the LLM?
correct_choice_text: The language-generating model inside the larger product.
correct_feedback: Good distinction. A product may wrap an LLM with tools, policies, and interface pieces.
choice_1_text: The language-generating model inside the larger product.
choice_1_feedback: Good distinction. A product may wrap an LLM with tools, policies, and interface pieces.
choice_2_text: The whole app, including every tool and button.
choice_2_feedback: Not quite. The product can include an LLM, but the LLM is not every surrounding tool and screen.
choice_3_text: The safety policy that blocks some outputs.
choice_3_feedback: Close, but a safety policy shapes behavior around the model; it is not the language model itself.
choice_4_text: The database or search index the app may call.
choice_4_feedback: This describes a retrieval or search component. The LLM generates language from context and learned weights.

card_objective: Compare rules-first AI and learned-pattern AI while recognizing that modern systems can combine rules, retrieval, policies, tools, and learned models.
stem: If a system solves a task only by following hand-written if-then rules, how is it different from an LLM?
correct_choice_text: The rule-based system applies explicit rules; an LLM’s fluency mostly comes from learned weights shaped by examples.
correct_feedback: Good distinction. LLMs mostly belong to the learned-patterns tradition, even when products around them use rules.
choice_1_text: The rule-based system applies explicit rules; an LLM’s fluency mostly comes from learned weights shaped by examples.
choice_1_feedback: Good distinction. LLMs mostly belong to the learned-patterns tradition, even when products around them use rules.
choice_2_text: The rule-based system measures loss and updates weights after every task.
choice_2_feedback: Not quite. Loss and weight updates belong to training learned models, not to a pure if-then rule system.
choice_3_text: The LLM has conscious reasoning, while the rule-based system has none.
choice_3_feedback: That gives the LLM too much mind. The key boundary here is learned weights versus explicit rules.
choice_4_text: There is no difference; both systems are just written rulebooks.
choice_4_feedback: Close, but LLM behavior is not mainly written as explicit if-then rules; it is shaped by training.
review_notes: Shorten the correct answer or lengthen/tighten distractors so the correct choice is not obviously more complete.

card_objective: Compare rules-first AI and learned-pattern AI while recognizing that modern systems can combine rules, retrieval, policies, tools, and learned models.
stem: During deep-learning training, why does the system measure loss?
correct_choice_text: Loss measures prediction error so an optimizer can adjust weights.
correct_feedback: Insight strengthened. Loss is the numerical training signal, not a feeling or truth meter.
choice_1_text: Loss measures prediction error so an optimizer can adjust weights.
choice_1_feedback: Insight strengthened. Loss is the numerical training signal, not a feeling or truth meter.
choice_2_text: Loss tells the model whether a generated answer is morally good.
choice_2_feedback: Not quite. Loss is a numerical error signal; alignment and policy questions need additional methods.
choice_3_text: Loss is the model’s confidence score for the next token during generation.
choice_3_feedback: Close, but confidence or probability shapes generation. Loss measures training error so weights can be adjusted.
choice_4_text: Loss writes the final answer after the model has finished thinking.
choice_4_feedback: Not quite. Loss is used during training; it is not the model writing a response.

card_objective: Compare rules-first AI and learned-pattern AI while recognizing that modern systems can combine rules, retrieval, policies, tools, and learned models.
stem: A university AI app combines an LLM, hand-written policy checks, and retrieval from a handbook. What should a model-literate user infer?
correct_choice_text: The product combines learned patterns, rules, and tools; the LLM inside is still a learned-pattern model.
correct_feedback: Good distinction. Modern AI products can mix traditions without making the model itself rule-based.
choice_1_text: The product combines learned patterns, rules, and tools; the LLM inside is still a learned-pattern model.
choice_1_feedback: Good distinction. Modern AI products can mix traditions without making the model itself rule-based.
choice_2_text: The hand-written checks turn the LLM into symbolic AI.
choice_2_feedback: That confuses a product wrapper with the model inside it.
choice_3_text: The retrieved handbook permanently rewrites the model weights.
choice_3_feedback: Retrieval can add context without becoming durable training.
choice_4_text: The policy checks prove the model has moral understanding.
choice_4_feedback: Policy behavior is not the same as conscience or awareness.
review_notes: Shorten the correct answer or lengthen/tighten distractors so the correct choice is not obviously more complete. Either replace future-stage jargon with plain language or add a brief explanation in the stem/feedback.

card_objective: Trace training as example, prediction, loss, optimizer, weight update, repeat; separate durable training from inference, retrieval, sampling, and prompt changes.
stem: During training, the model predicts a target token and gets it wrong. What makes that moment learning rather than ordinary inference?
correct_choice_text: The training process uses the loss signal to update weights.
correct_feedback: Good distinction. The durable weight update is what makes this training.
choice_1_text: The training process uses the loss signal to update weights.
choice_1_feedback: Good distinction. The durable weight update is what makes this training.
choice_2_text: The model appends the mistaken token to a normal live response.
choice_2_feedback: Not quite. Appending a response token is inference behavior; training uses loss to change weights.
choice_3_text: The prompt becomes durable memory stored inside the model’s weights.
choice_3_feedback: This choice reveals a common mix-up. Context can shape the current chat without becoming a weight update.
choice_4_text: The model treats the most likely answer as automatically true.
choice_4_feedback: Not quite. Likelihood is not truth, and this training moment matters because loss can drive a weight update.

card_objective: Trace training as example, prediction, loss, optimizer, weight update, repeat; separate durable training from inference, retrieval, sampling, and prompt changes.
stem: A student asks a chatbot one question during ordinary use. Which statement is usually true?
correct_choice_text: The model uses fixed weights and temporary context; the chat usually does not rewrite the weights.
correct_feedback: Insight strengthened. Normal chat inference can use context, but it usually does not rewrite weights.
choice_1_text: The model uses fixed weights and temporary context; the chat usually does not rewrite the weights.
choice_1_feedback: Insight strengthened. Normal chat inference can use context, but it usually does not rewrite weights.
choice_2_text: The chat permanently updates the base model’s weights.
choice_2_feedback: Not quite. That would require a training process, not ordinary inference.
choice_3_text: The model stores the prompt as durable memory inside its weights.
choice_3_feedback: This choice reveals a common mix-up. Context can shape the current chat without becoming a weight update.
choice_4_text: The optimizer trains the model after every response.
choice_4_feedback: Close, but optimizers update weights during training, not after every normal response.
review_notes: Shorten the correct answer or lengthen/tighten distractors so the correct choice is not obviously more complete.

card_objective: Trace training as example, prediction, loss, optimizer, weight update, repeat; separate durable training from inference, retrieval, sampling, and prompt changes.
stem: Why can training change how the model behaves tomorrow, while inference usually cannot?
correct_choice_text: Training changes parameters that future runs reuse; inference uses temporary computations for the current run.
correct_feedback: Good distinction. Durable parameters carry forward; inference-time states usually do not.
choice_1_text: Training changes parameters that future runs reuse; inference uses temporary computations for the current run.
choice_1_feedback: Good distinction. Durable parameters carry forward; inference-time states usually do not.
choice_2_text: Inference is where the optimizer changes weights for future runs.
choice_2_feedback: Not quite. Optimizer-driven weight updates belong to training, not ordinary inference.
choice_3_text: Training only changes the visible prompt text for one response.
choice_3_feedback: Close, but prompt changes are temporary input changes; training changes learned numbers.
choice_4_text: Training guarantees that every future answer will be true.
choice_4_feedback: This choice overclaims. Training can improve behavior, but it does not guarantee truth.

card_objective: Trace training as example, prediction, loss, optimizer, weight update, repeat; separate durable training from inference, retrieval, sampling, and prompt changes.
stem: In a training loop, which sequence is the durable learning path?
correct_choice_text: Example -> prediction -> loss -> weight update.
correct_feedback: Insight strengthened. That is the weight-changing path.
choice_1_text: Example -> prediction -> loss -> weight update.
choice_1_feedback: Insight strengthened. That is the weight-changing path.
choice_2_text: Prompt -> response token -> append -> next response token.
choice_2_feedback: Close, but that sequence describes inference/generation, not training.
choice_3_text: Search -> outside passage -> current context -> answer.
choice_3_feedback: That describes a search or retrieval step. It can add temporary context; it does not by itself update weights.
choice_4_text: User correction -> better answer -> permanent learning inside the same chat.
choice_4_feedback: Not quite. A correction can steer the current conversation, but durable learning requires a training process.

card_objective: Explain pretraining as broad durable pattern learning from many next-token targets, not perfect recall, live browsing, or a searchable archive.
stem: During pretraining, what signal teaches the model broad language patterns across many examples?
correct_choice_text: Repeated prediction error/loss from next-token targets changes weights over many training steps.
correct_feedback: Good distinction. Pretraining scales the training loop across many examples.
choice_1_text: Repeated prediction error/loss from next-token targets changes weights over many training steps.
choice_1_feedback: Good distinction. Pretraining scales the training loop across many examples.
choice_2_text: A user conversation at deployment permanently rewrites the model.
choice_2_feedback: Not quite. Pretraining happens before normal use; an ordinary chat usually does not rewrite weights.
choice_3_text: A human writes every rule the model will follow.
choice_3_feedback: Close, but pretraining is learned from examples, not a complete rulebook written by hand.
choice_4_text: The model stores a perfect copy of every source document.
choice_4_feedback: This choice reveals a common mix-up. Pretraining shapes weights; it is not perfect document storage.

card_objective: Explain pretraining as broad durable pattern learning from many next-token targets, not perfect recall, live browsing, or a searchable archive.
stem: Why can pretraining create broad capability without making the model a perfect library of its sources?
correct_choice_text: It shapes statistical patterns in weights; it does not create a searchable copy of every source.
correct_feedback: Good distinction. Pretraining can shape broad ability without turning the model into a perfect source library.
choice_1_text: It shapes statistical patterns in weights; it does not create a searchable copy of every source.
choice_1_feedback: Good distinction. Pretraining can shape broad ability without turning the model into a perfect source library.
choice_2_text: It gives the model direct access to every private file on demand.

choice_3_text: It inserts the original source into every future prompt context.
choice_3_feedback: Close, but adding source text to context is different from pretraining. Pretraining changes weights before use.
choice_4_text: It happens only after a user asks a specific question.
choice_4_feedback: Pretraining happens before deployment. The user prompt later uses the pretrained weights.

card_objective: Explain pretraining as broad durable pattern learning from many next-token targets, not perfect recall, live browsing, or a searchable archive.

choice_1_feedback: Good distinction. Pretraining changes reusable model structure before later prompts arrive.

card_objective: Explain pretraining as broad durable pattern learning from many next-token targets, not perfect recall, live browsing, or a searchable archive.

card_objective: Diagnose overfitting and explain that set-aside validation examples are kept out of training to test transfer beyond examples used to fit weights.
stem: A model gets every training example right but fails on new validation examples. What problem is showing up?
correct_choice_text: Overfitting: the model fit training examples too narrowly instead of learning patterns that generalize.
correct_feedback: Good diagnosis. Set-aside validation examples are saved for testing, not used to fit the model.
choice_1_text: Overfitting: the model fit training examples too narrowly instead of learning patterns that generalize.
choice_1_feedback: Good diagnosis. Set-aside validation examples are saved for testing, not used to fit the model.
choice_2_text: Generalization: the model is working well on unseen cases.
choice_2_feedback: Not quite. Generalization means good performance on new examples; this scenario shows the opposite.
choice_3_text: Better training: the model has clearly learned the general rule.
choice_3_feedback: Not quite. If it fails on new validation examples, high training performance is not enough evidence of generalization.
choice_4_text: Inference: the model is simply generating one token at a time.
choice_4_feedback: Close, but inference is normal model use; overfitting is a training/evaluation failure mode.
review_notes: Shorten the correct answer or lengthen/tighten distractors so the correct choice is not obviously more complete.

card_objective: Diagnose overfitting and explain that set-aside validation examples are kept out of training to test transfer beyond examples used to fit weights.
stem: Why do model builders test on set-aside validation examples instead of only training examples?
correct_choice_text: They are kept out of training so they can test whether learned patterns transfer.
correct_feedback: Insight strengthened. Set-aside validation examples are saved for testing, not used to fit the model.
choice_1_text: They are kept out of training so they can test whether learned patterns transfer.
choice_1_feedback: Insight strengthened. Set-aside validation examples are saved for testing, not used to fit the model.
choice_2_text: Validation data is just extra training data used to lower loss.
choice_2_feedback: Not quite. Validation examples are saved for testing, not used as extra examples to fit the model.
choice_3_text: Training examples alone prove the model will work everywhere.
choice_3_feedback: This choice reveals the exact overfitting risk. Seen examples are not enough evidence.
choice_4_text: A lower loss always proves every future answer is true.
choice_4_feedback: Close, but lower loss is not a truth guarantee, especially outside the tested examples.

card_objective: Diagnose overfitting and explain that set-aside validation examples are kept out of training to test transfer beyond examples used to fit weights.
stem: A model trained heavily on one narrow answer template works on that template but struggles with varied new prompts. What should a model-literate learner suspect?
correct_choice_text: It may have fit the narrow examples too closely instead of learning a pattern that generalizes.
correct_feedback: Good distinction. Targeted training can help, but narrow training can also make behavior brittle.

choice_1_feedback: Good distinction. Targeted training can help, but narrow training can also make behavior brittle.
choice_2_text: The prompts permanently rewrote the model during inference.
choice_2_feedback: Not quite. The issue is training behavior, not prompts rewriting weights during inference.
choice_3_text: The model is definitely conscious but choosing not to answer.

choice_4_text: Rule-based AI is always better than learned models.
choice_4_feedback: This overgeneralizes. The issue is narrow training behavior, not proof that rules always beat learned models.
